mnemonics.title: M.E.R.G.E - Memory Aid for Merge Sort Steps
mnemonics.content: M - Midpoint finding (divide array in half), E - Extract (recursively sort both halves), R - Rejoin (merge sorted halves), G - Gather (combine in sorted order), E - Everyone sorted (complete)

mnemonics.title: Q.U.I.C.K - Quick Sort Process
mnemonics.content: Q - Choose Pivot, U - Unite (partition around pivot), I - Items left of pivot (smaller), C - Items right of pivot (larger), K - Keep recursing until sorted

mnemonics.title: 2 Halves + Merge = MERGE SORT
mnemonics.content: Think: 'To sort an array, CUT it in HALVES, sort each HALF, then MERGE them back together' - emphasizes the divide, conquer, combine pattern

mnemonics.title: Pivot Power for Quick Sort
mnemonics.content: Remember: 'Pick a PIVOT, PARTITION left (smaller) and right (larger), then REPEAT on sub-arrays' - pivot choice determines efficiency

mnemonics.title: Stable vs Unstable
mnemonics.content: Merge Sort = 'Mostly STABLE because it MAINTAINS relative order of equal elements' ; Quick Sort = 'QUICK but Questionably stable' (unstable)

mnemonics.title: Time Complexity Tale
mnemonics.content: Merge Sort: 'Most Likely LOG N' (always O(n log n)) ; Quick Sort: 'Quickly GOOD, QUICKLY BAD' (O(n log n) avg, O(n²) worst)

acronyms.usage: Both Merge Sort and Quick Sort use this paradigm - divide problem into subproblems, conquer (solve), combine results
Used in recurrence relations: Merge Sort T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n), Quick Sort T(n) = T(k) + T(n-k-1) + O(n)
Best/Average case for both Merge Sort and Quick Sort; indicates efficient sorting
Worst case for Quick Sort (when pivot is always smallest/largest element); never occurs in Merge Sort
Quick Sort is in-place (O(log n) stack), Merge Sort is not in-place (O(n) extra space needed for merging)

acronyms.expansion: Preserves relative order of equal elements
acronyms.usage: Merge Sort is stable; Quick Sort is unstable - important when sorting objects with multiple fields

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an inverted tree: root = full array, branches split into two halves (depth = log n), leaves = single elements; then merge bottom-up combining smaller sorted lists into larger ones
Visualize a line of people where you pick one person (pivot) and ask everyone shorter to stand on their LEFT and taller to stand on their RIGHT - now pivot is in final sorted position
Think of two sorted piles of cards face-up: always pick the smaller top card and place it in a new pile - like merging two sorted sequences while maintaining order
Imagine array already sorted [1,2,3,4,5] and you always pick first element as pivot - you create partitions of size n-1 and 0 each time, leading to O(n²); visualize staircase breakdown
Merge Sort = needs a big empty room (O(n) extra space); Quick Sort = only needs small corners (O(log n) stack space) - this is why Quick Sort preferred for memory-constrained systems

analogies.analogy: Think of two sorted decks of cards on a table. To merge them, you compare the top card of each deck, pick the smaller one, and place it in a new pile. Repeat until both decks are empty. This is exactly how Merge Sort combines two sorted subarrays.

Imagine students lining up randomly. You pick one student as a 'pivot' (say 5 feet tall). All students shorter than 5 feet stand to their LEFT, all taller stand to RIGHT. The pivot is now in correct position. Repeat this process in each subgroup.

Like cleaning a messy room: instead of tackling whole room at once, you divide it into sections (divide), clean each section thoroughly (conquer), then arrange all sections together in order (combine).

analogies.concept: Merge Sort Stability - Library Book Sorting
analogies.analogy: Imagine sorting library books first by author (alphabetically), then by title. If two books have same author, they maintain their title order. Merge Sort preserves this; Quick Sort might mix up the title order within same author.

analogies.concept: Quick Sort Pivot - Finding Seat in Theater
analogies.analogy: Finding a seat in a crowded theater: you pick a row (pivot), ask people to shift so those who should sit before your row go left, those after go right - you found where your row belongs, now repeat for each section.

analogies.concept: In-Place vs External Space - Desk vs Storage Room
analogies.analogy: Quick Sort works like organizing papers on your desk (minimal extra space needed); Merge Sort is like needing a separate storage room to lay out all papers before reorganizing them (requires O(n) extra space).
